# Transcrição: 09-Library of Stories

## [00:00 - 03:03]

For someone who's potentially not a storyteller, if I asked you right now and I said, tell me five stories about you, go. You probably freak out and you probably said to me, ah, Vin, I don't have any stories to tell you. That's not a truthful answer. The truthful answer is actually, ah, Vin, I don't document my stories and as a result I'm relying on my memory to remember it and if I'm relying on my memory right now I don't remember any of them. That's the real answer. Most people don't develop the habit of documenting stories and then as a result they don't storytell. This is why as you learn the library of stories or how to build a library of stories, this is when you'll start to understand that documentation is a huge part of becoming a great storyteller. So let's once and for all learn this great habit so that we can become great storytellers once and for all. The first step when it comes to building a library of stories is called Eye of the Writer. Now in life, I don't know if you suffer from this but I do, in life sometimes one day blends into another, one week blends into the other, one month blends into another and then one year kind of blends into the other and then all of a sudden everything's just a blur. That happens to me. Sometimes I get stuck in a mode where I'm living life on autopilot and when I'm asking you to engage in Eye of the Writer, what I'm actually asking you to do is become more emotionally aware of what moves you in life. Become more emotionally aware of what moves you in life. Now when you engage in Eye of the Writer and you become more emotionally aware, when something moves you, document it. Document it. And when you document things from this point in your life moving forward into the future, that allows you to document stories moving forward. So you capture the stories into the future represented by the F. Now of course if you just make the commitment of capturing one story per week, if you make that your goal, then by the end of this year you have 20, 30, 40, 50 stories if you just commit to capturing one story a week. And when something emotionally moves you, when you capture it, what you do is you do something like this. What you currently see is one of my templates. I use this template when I'm documenting stories and you can use any note taking app you want. But for me the moment something emotionally moves me, I then fill in the blanks. Notice how I've got the incident there and under the incident I've got the who, what, where and when, Vax, visual, auditory, kinesthetic and smell. Jot down a few quick notes and then I even write down the dialogue, key things that were said that really emotionally moved me. And then right on the bottom I even write down a few points. What could this story be about? It takes me five to ten minutes to document this story.

## [03:03 - 06:05]

But the moment something moves me, I document it like you just saw. And again, once I've done that, I'm capturing stories moving forward into the future. Now what about the past though? Because there are a bunch of stories in your past. You've been through thousands of experiences in your life. Some of them exciting, some of them thrilling, some of them sad, some of them maybe boring. But within these experiences that make up you as a person, embedded in those experiences are stories. Now we need to be able to look to the past for the stories. Now one of the ways to look to the past, I call this the facts roll strategy. The facts roll strategy. And that allows you to capture stories into the past. This way you capture stories as a habit moving forward into the future. But you can work on one exercise that can help you harvest stories from the past. Now I know the facts roll strategy isn't the most eloquent name. However, the ingredients you need to execute this strategy to harvest the stories from the past is you need a facts roll. And a facts roll is just a roll of paper. And a facts machine is just a machine we used to use in the ancient past when we're communicating. Don't worry about what it is. But just get a facts roll of paper or a roll of paper. Then you also need lots of coloured crayons to bring out the inner child. And then you also need some people that have known you for a while. So whether it's friends or family, try to grab two to three people that have known you the longest. Because that will make the strategy the most effective. Once you have these ingredients, take the roll of paper and then you roll it out. You roll it all the way across the room. Now on one side, on this side of the facts roll or on this side of the roll of paper, write down the day you were born. So birth date. Draw a really long line all the way across to over here and draw another line and then write present day. And then in between, start making a few markings. Over here write earliest memory. First time you had a crush in junior primary school or in primary school. Then when you went to high school, maybe a first job. First time you had a kiss. First time you got into a job and then you got fired. Next time you got detention. So mark down those key areas. First real professional job. University. And just mark down all the key errors of your life. And if you need help during this section, check out this here on screen. As you look at this, these are the things that will help you jolt some ideas. And I'll give you a moment to take a screenshot of this because I think this is very useful. I find it useful. You know, think of places, you know, even put on some music that reminds you of that era of life. It usually brings back a lot of memories. Now once you've done this and you've marked it all out, give all of your friends a crayon. And if you're old enough, maybe have a little bit of wine. It might help with the experience. And then say to your friends, Hey, do you remember any stories or any experiences that we've had together that you remember of me? And then you're going to have friends say to you, Oh, do you remember that time?

Eye of the writer, engage in it more, document it when something emotionally moves you, capture stories into the future. Use the fact story strategy to capture stories in the past. The second step is called eye of the V slash P. Eye of the V slash P. And what I mean by V slash P is videographer and photographer. Eye of the videographer and eye of the photographer. When I started working with more videographers, they had this great line and Craig always used to say this to me. He'd say to me, what you can show, don't tell. What you can show, don't tell. We live in the modern world now and we no longer have to rely just on our voice to tell stories. We can bring the characters to life by showing a photo and also showing a video. ## [09:05 - 11:49]

How connected did you feel to that accountant? You probably didn't feel that connected to him. That's because I didn't even show you a photo of him. I didn't show you a video. Now I have photos and I have videos, but I didn't show you because I wanted to illustrate this example. You probably didn't feel that connected to the accountant. However, how much more connected did you feel to my grandmother? Did you feel more connected to her? You probably did because I showed you videos and photos of my grandmother. I was able to successfully bring her to life for you so you were better able to connect with her in that story. Can you see the importance of eye of the videographer and eye of the photographer? So when you can, when you're documenting the story, like you can see in this example here, in this example, when I was documenting this particular story, if you look over there, I've also documented the photos and the videos. Now depending on what note taking app you use, a lot of them are going to allow you to store text and images and videos all in the same notes file. This means that anytime you want to access this story, it has the incident all there, all the ingredients, it's got the points, and it also has the photos and videos all in one place for you to be able to access, which now brings us to the third step in the formula, which is eye of the director, eye of the director. And with eye of the director, that's when you're sitting down with the lens of a director and you're going, hmm, what could this story symbolize? Does this sound familiar? Remember when we're storytelling, we write down the story and then we write down all the words that the story could symbolize. This is what you do with eye of the director. So you sit down with your particular story that you've documented with the incident, the point, the pictures and the videos, and then you start to write down some of the words that this story could symbolize. That is you engaging in eye of the director. Because the director, they always look at stories and they go, which angle can I tell this story from? Like if it's a horror movie, a murder story, should I tell it from the murderer's point of view? Should I tell it from the neighbor's point of view? Should I tell it from the children's point of view and the family that got murdered? Or should I tell it from the parent's point of view? So to use such a dark example. But that's what a director does. And that's what you do. The very next time someone wants you to do a presentation, you sit down and you engage with eye of the director and you write down all the words that the story could symbolize. And there you now have all the ingredients and all the preparation is now done for you to become a better storyteller. People often don't storytell because they don't document it. Now you have the documentation process. I hope this helps you on your journey of becoming a masterful storyteller.
